title: Postpartum Hemorrhage
objectives: Define PPH and recognize risk factors
Apply the 4 T's framework for etiology
Know when to escalate to surgical intervention

vignette.text:
A 32-year-old G3P3 delivered vaginally 30 minutes ago after a prolonged labor with oxytocin augmentation. The placenta delivered intact. She is now having heavy vaginal bleeding and appears pale. Estimated blood loss is 800 mL and ongoing.

Delivery History: Term infant, 4200g birth weight, prolonged second stage

physicalExam.abdomen: Uterus soft, boggy, fundus above umbilicus
physicalExam.vaginal: Continued bleeding, no obvious lacerations on inspection

questions.question: What defines postpartum hemorrhage?
options: >500mL after vaginal delivery or >1000mL after cesarean
questions.explanation: Traditional definition: >500mL vaginal, >1000mL cesarean. Newer definition (ACOG): cumulative blood loss ≥1000mL with signs/symptoms of hypovolemia within 24h regardless of delivery route.

questions.question: Using the 4 T's, what is the most likely cause in this patient?
questions.explanation: Boggy uterus = uterine atony (Tone). Risk factors present: prolonged labor, oxytocin use, macrosomia. Atony is the #1 cause of PPH (~80%).

questions.question: What is first-line treatment for uterine atony?
questions.explanation: First-line: fundal massage + uterotonics (oxytocin, methylergonovine, carboprost, misoprostol). Bimanual compression while uterotonics take effect. Escalate if no response.

questions.question: Which uterotonic is contraindicated in asthma?
questions.explanation: Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) causes bronchospasm - contraindicated in asthma. Methylergonovine is contraindicated in hypertension. Know the contraindications!

keyTakeaways: Atony: boggy uterus - treat with massage + uterotonics
Carboprost contraindicated in asthma; methylergonovine in HTN
Escalation: Bakri balloon → B-Lynch suture → uterine artery ligation/embolization → hysterectomy
Massive transfusion protocol if ongoing hemorrhage
